Pandemic Name: Italian Plague of 1629–1631, Great Plague of Milan
Total Death Toll: At least 280,000 deaths, estimates up to one million
Means of Transmission: Carried through Europe by troop movements, spread through Milan
Significant Impacts: Contributed to economic decline, high fatalities in Milan and Venice
Affected Regions: Northern and central Italy, Lombardy, Venice, Bologna, Tyrol
Potential Origin: Northern France in 1623, brought to Lombardy by soldiers
Potential Sources: Troop movements during the War of the Mantuan Succession

Pandemic Name: Persian Plague epidemic of 1772–1773, Persian Plague
Means of Transmission: Bubonic plague, spread throughout the Persian Empire, reached Basra and Bahrain, extended to Bombay, India
Significant Impacts: Introduction of quarantine measures in the Persian Gulf regions, devastating outbreak, daily deaths recorded in the thousands
Affected Regions: Persian Empire, Persian Gulf regions, Basra, Bahrain, Bombay, India
Containment Measures: Imposed quarantine measures, positive effects seen by the end of 1773

Total Death Toll: Over one million deaths in Russia, 10,000 deaths in London in 1853-54, 23,000 deaths in Great Britain in 1854
Means of Transmission: Contaminated water, identified by John Snow in London
Significant Impacts: High fatalities in Asia, Europe, Africa, and North America, John Snow's breakthrough in identifying water as the transmission source

Means of Transmission: Spread facilitated by modern transport infrastructure
Significant Impacts: Among the deadliest pandemics in history, global spread, recurrences in subsequent years
Affected Regions: Central Asia, European countries, Russian Empire, worldwide spread
Potential Pathogen: Possibly caused by human coronavirus OC43

Pandemic Name: 1957–1958 Asian flu pandemic, H2N2 influenza A virus
Means of Transmission: Spread throughout China, later globally, initial cases in Guizhou and Yunnan
Significant Impacts: One of the deadliest pandemics in history, led to the Hong Kong flu pandemic, establishment of Chinese National Influenza Center

Total Death Toll: Approximately 1/3 of the entire Japanese population
Means of Transmission: Carried by a Japanese fisherman from the Korean peninsula, spread rapidly throughout northern Kyushu, then to eastern Japan and Nara
Significant Impacts: Significant social, economic, and religious repercussions, poor agricultural yields, famine, tax exemption extended to all of Japan
Affected Regions: Northern Kyushu, eastern Japan, Nara, entire Japan

Total Death Toll: Estimated 5–10 million deaths, 10% of the Roman Empire's population
Means of Transmission: Possibly contracted and spread by returning soldiers, smallpox or measles
Significant Impacts: Potentially claimed the life of Roman Emperor Lucius Verus, high death toll in cities and Roman army
Affected Regions: Roman Empire, Gaul, legions along the Rhine, city of Rome

Means of Transmission: Spread by fleas carrying Yersinia pestis, person-to-person pneumonic plague
Significant Impacts: Religious, social, and economic upheavals, profound effects on European history
Affected Regions: Afro-Eurasia, Europe, North Africa, Middle East
Potential Origin: Central or East Asia, first appearance in Crimea in 1347
Potential Sources: Originated in Central or East Asia, spread through Mediterranean Basin, carried by fleas on black rats on Genoese ships

Means of Transmission: Airborne droplets and particles, contaminated surfaces
Significant Impacts: More than 500 million cases, one of the deadliest pandemics in history, global spread
'Other variants: Mutations have produced many strains with varying degrees of infectivity and virulence

Means of Transmission: Descended from H2N2 through antigenic shift, first recorded in Hong Kong, possible origin in mainland China
Significant Impacts: Among the deadliest pandemics in history, low mortality rate, mild clinical symptoms
Affected Regions: Global impact, particularly in Hong Kong, mainland China

Total Death Toll: Up to 1,250,000 deaths throughout the Kingdom of Naples
Means of Transmission: Spread through central and southern Italy, reached Rome and Liguria
Significant Impacts: Severe economic and social consequences, high fatality rates in Naples, Rome, and Genoa
Affected Regions: Central and southern Italy, Kingdom of Naples
Potential Origin: Possibly originated in Algiers, spread to Sardinia, then Naples
Containment Measures: Forced quarantine in affected districts, efforts of Martinus Ludheim

Total Death Toll: Approximately a fifth of Constantinople's population, millions globally
Means of Transmission: Spread through Mediterranean, Europe, Near East, Yersinia pestis
Significant Impacts: Affected Byzantine and Sasanian Empires, severe impact on Constantinople
Affected Regions: Mediterranean Basin, Europe, Near East, Sasanian Empire, Byzantine Empire
Potential Origin: Tian Shan region near Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan, China
Potential Sources: Yersinia pestis strain found in Tian Shan, Procopius and other historical accounts

Pandemic Name: 1918 influenza pandemic, Spanish flu, Great Influenza epidemic
Means of Transmission: Spread through four successive waves globally, exact origin uncertain
Significant Impacts: Second deadliest pandemic in history, high mortality in young adults, cytokine storm effect, exacerbated by war conditions
Potential Factors: Climate anomaly affecting disease vectors, bacterial superinfection due to malnourishment and poor hygiene

Total Death Toll: Over 12 million deaths globally, with at least 10 million in India
Means of Transmission: Spread through ocean-going trade, transporting infected persons, rats, and cargoes harboring fleas, person-to-person contagion for the more virulent strain
Significant Impacts: One of the deadliest pandemics in history, active until 1960, continued lower levels of plague deaths annually
Affected Regions: All inhabited continents, particularly India and China
Potential Sources: Waves from two sources - primarily bubonic spread globally, more virulent strain confined to Asia, particularly Manchuria and Mongolia
